steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: When light travels from air into glass, it slows down. What happens to it?
options: It bends away from the normal
Nothing — it continues in a straight line
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! When light enters a denser medium (like glass) it slows down and bends TOWARDS the normal. It bends AWAY from the normal only when going into a less dense medium (e.g. glass to air). Remember: slower = towards the normal.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! When light enters a denser medium it slows down and bends towards the normal. The rule is: entering a denser medium → bends towards the normal; entering a less dense medium → bends away.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA Physics specification 6.1.2 — Reflection and refraction

steps.title: Reflection & Refraction
steps.bodyHTML: Reflection is when a wave bounces off a surface. The law of reflection states: the angle of incidence = angle of reflection. Both angles are measured from the normal (an imaginary line perpendicular to the surface). Refraction is when a wave changes direction because it changes speed at a boundary between two materials. When a wave enters a denser medium, it slows down and bends towards the normal. When it enters a less dense medium, it speeds up and bends away from the normal. The frequency stays the same — only the speed and wavelength change.
steps.keyEquation: angle of incidence = angle of reflection

relationships: Angle of incidence = angle of reflection
Denser medium → slower → bends towards normal
Less dense medium → faster → bends away from normal

commonErrors: Measuring angles from the surface instead of the normal
Thinking the frequency changes during refraction (it does not)

steps.title: See it in action
steps.instruction: Drag the slider to change the angle of incidence. Watch how the reflected and refracted rays behave.

aqa.questionText: A ray of light hits a plane mirror at an angle of 35° to the mirror surface. Calculate the angle of reflection.

steps.label: Find the angle of incidence
steps.content:
Angles are measured from the normal, not the surface.
Angle to surface = 35°, so angle to normal = 90° − 35° = 55°
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — converting surface angle to normal angle

steps.label: Apply the law of reflection
steps.content: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence:
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — law of reflection stated; 1 mark — correct answer

edexcel.questionText: Explain why a light ray bends towards the normal when it enters a glass block from air.

steps.label: State what happens to speed
steps.content: Light slows down when entering glass because glass is denser than air.

steps.label: Explain the bending
steps.content: Because the light slows down at the boundary, it bends towards the normal.

steps.label: What stays the same
steps.content: The frequency of the light does not change — only the speed and wavelength change.

edexcel.finalAnswer: Light slows in glass (denser medium) and bends towards the normal. Frequency stays the same.

steps.label: State the law
steps.content: The angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection.

steps.label: Clarify measurement
steps.content: Both angles are measured from the normal (perpendicular to the surface).

ocr-gateway.finalAnswer: The angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection, both measured from the normal.

ocr-21c.questionText: A light ray passes from glass into air. Describe what happens to its speed and direction.

steps.label: Speed change
steps.content: Light speeds up when leaving glass and entering air (less dense medium).

steps.label: Direction change
steps.content: The ray bends away from the normal.

ocr-21c.finalAnswer: Light speeds up and bends away from the normal when passing from glass to air.

wjec.questionText: A light ray hits a mirror at 40° to the normal. What is the angle of reflection?

steps.label: Apply the law of reflection
steps.content: The angle is already measured from the normal, so:
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — law of reflection; 1 mark — correct answer

aqa.questionText: A light ray hits a plane mirror. The angle between the ray and the mirror surface is 25°. What is the angle of reflection?
aqa.commonMistake: Option A assumes the angle to the surface equals the angle of reflection — but angles must be from the normal.

edexcel.questionText: What happens to light when it enters a less dense medium?

options.label: A
options.text: It slows down and bends towards the normal

options.label: B
options.text: It speeds up and bends away from the normal

options.label: C
options.text: It speeds up and bends towards the normal

options.label: D
options.text: It slows down and bends away from the normal

markSchemeSteps: Less dense → speeds up → bends away from normal ✓

ocr-gateway.questionText: During refraction, which property of a wave does NOT change?

ocr-21c.questionText: Light enters glass from air. Which direction does it bend?
markSchemeSteps: Air to glass = less dense to denser → bends towards normal ✓

wjec.questionText: The angle of incidence is 30°. What is the angle of reflection?
markSchemeSteps: Law of reflection: angle of incidence = angle of reflection = 30° ✓

equation.canonical: angle of incidence = angle of reflection
equation.examTip: ALWAYS measure from the normal, never from the surface. If given an angle to the surface, subtract it from 90° first.

boardNotes.aqa: Must know the law of reflection and explain refraction in terms of speed change.
boardNotes.edexcel: Draw ray diagrams accurately with a ruler. Label the normal as a dashed line.
boardNotes.ocr-gateway: Know that frequency stays constant during refraction. Speed and wavelength change.
boardNotes.ocr-21c: Link refraction to everyday examples: swimming pools looking shallower, lenses.
boardNotes.wjec: Be able to draw and label ray diagrams for both reflection and refraction.
